volumeTitle: Casina. The Casket Comedy. Curculio. Epidicus. The Two Menaechmuses
label: Epidicus (Section 3.4.495 - Section 4.1.555b)

sections.label: Section 3.4.495
sections.original: mercatus te hodie est de lenone Apoecides ?
sections.translation: Did Apaecides purchase you to-day of the procurer ? ( A pause .) Come now , tell me .

sections.label: Section 3.4.496
sections.original: Fando ego istunc hominem numquam audivi ante hunc diem ,
sections.translation: I never heard of that person before to-day , nor , indeed , was any one able to purchase me for any money ; I ’ ve been free now for more than five years .

sections.label: Section 3.4.499
sections.original: Quid tibi negotist meae domi igitur ?
sections.translation: What business have you , then , at my house ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.499b
sections.translation: You shall hear ;

sections.label: Section 3.4.500
sections.original: conducta veni , ut fidibus cantarem seni ,
sections.translation: I came , being hired to perform for an old gentleman while he was sacrificing .

sections.label: Section 3.4.501b
sections.translation: I do confess that I am the most worthless of all men in Athens of Attica . But do you know Acropolistis the Music-girl ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.504
sections.translation: As well as my own self .

sections.label: Section 3.4.504b
sections.translation: Where does she live ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.504c
sections.translation: Since she has been made free ,

sections.label: Section 3.4.505
sections.original: ubi habitet dicere admodum incerte scio .
sections.translation: I don ’ t know for certain .

sections.label: Section 3.4.506
sections.original: Eho an libera illa est ? quis eam liberaverit ,
sections.translation: Well now , I should like to know who has made her free , if you know ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.507b
sections.translation: That which I have heard , you shall hear ; I heard that Stratippocles [*] , the son of Periphanes , had provided in his absence that she should be made free .

sections.label: Section 3.4.510
sections.original: Perii hercle , si istaec vera sunt ; planissume
sections.translation: By heavens , I ’ m undone [*] , most clearly , if these things are true . Epidicus has disembowelled my purse !

sections.label: Section 3.4.512
sections.original: Haec sic audivi . numquid me vis ceterum ?
sections.translation: I ’ ve heard to that effect . Do you want me for anything else ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.513
sections.original: Malo cruciatu ut pereas atque abeas cito .
sections.translation: Away to perdition in the veriest torments , and off this instant !

sections.label: Section 3.4.514
sections.translation: Won ’ t you give me back my harp ?

sections.label: Section 3.4.514b
sections.translation: Neither harp nor pipes .

sections.label: Section 3.4.515
sections.original: propera igitur fugere hinc , si te di amant .
sections.translation: Make haste , then , and escape from here , if the Gods love you !

sections.label: Section 3.4.515b
sections.translation: I ’ ll be off . At a future time , however , you ’ ll restore it , with the greater disgrace [*] to yourself .

sections.label: Section 3.4.517
sections.original: Quid nunc ? qui in tantis positus sum sententiis , †
sections.translation: What now ? Shall I , who have been placed before so many edicts [*] , allow him to get with impunity ? No ; even though as much again should be required to be lost , I ’ ll lose it rather than

sections.label: Section 3.4.520
sections.original: me impune irrisum esse , habitum depeculatui
sections.translation: allow myself to be held in derision with impunity and plundered by them . That I should have been thus cheated openly to my face , and that I should have been set at nought before this Apaecides , who is famed as being the framer and founder of all the laws and ordinances ! He too declares that he is a wise man ! that the hammer ,

sections.label: Section 3.4.525
sections.translation: forsooth , should be wiser than the handle [*] !

sections.label: Section 4.1.526
sections.original: Sí quid est homini míseriarum quód miserescat , míser ex animost .
sections.translation: If a mortal being has aught of misery , through which , miserable creature , to be wretched from the heart , that same do I experience , for whom full many a woe unites in the same spot , which , all , at the same instant are beating against my breast . A multitude of troubles keep me in suspense .

sections.label: Section 4.1.530
sections.translation: Poverty and misery alarm the thoughts of my heart ; nor have I anywhere a spot of safety where to fix my hopes ; in such a way has my daughter fallen into the power of the enemy [*] ; nor do I know where she now is .

sections.label: Section 4.1.533
sections.original: Quis illaec est mulier , timido pectore peregre adveniens
sections.translation: Who is this woman coming from a distance with a breast filled with alarms , and who thus bewails her lot ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.534b
sections.original: In his dictust locis habitare mihi
sections.translation: It was told me that Periphanes was living in this neighbourhood .

sections.label: Section 4.1.535b
sections.original: Me nominat haec ; credo ego illi hospitio usus venit .
sections.translation: She ’ s mentioning me ; need of hospitality has befallen her , I suppose .

sections.label: Section 4.1.536
sections.original: Pervelim mercedem dare , qui monstret eum mihi hominem aut ubi habitet .
sections.translation: I would be very willing to give a reward to any one who would point me out that man , or where he dwells .

sections.label: Section 4.1.537
sections.original: Noscito ego hanc , nam videor nescio ubi mi vidisse prius .
sections.translation: I recognize her ; for I think I have seen her before ; where , I know not . Is it , or is it not she , whom my mind suspects her to be ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.539
sections.translation: Good Gods ! I have seen this person before !

sections.label: Section 4.1.540
sections.original: Certo eást ⋯ quam in Épidauro paupérculam memini cómprimere .
sections.translation: It surely is she , a poor woman whom I remember having an intrigue with at Epidaurus .

sections.label: Section 4.1.541
sections.original: Plane hícine est , qui mihi in Épidauro primús pudicitiam pépulit .
sections.translation: Surely it is he , who at Epidaurus first violated my maiden modesty .

sections.label: Section 4.1.542
sections.original: Quaé meo compréssu peperit fíliam quam dómi nunc habeo .
sections.translation: She who had the daughter by me whom I ’ ve now got at home .

sections.label: Section 4.1.543
sections.translation: What if I accost him ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.543b
sections.translation: I don ’ t know whether to make up to her .

sections.label: Section 4.1.543c
sections.translation: If this is she —

sections.label: Section 4.1.543d
sections.translation: But if it is the man , as length of years renders me doubtful —

sections.label: Section 4.1.545
sections.original: Lónga dies meum incértat animum . sín east quam incerte aútumo ,
sections.translation: Length of time renders my mind uncertain . But if it is she , whom with some doubt I conjecture it to be , I ’ ll accost her circumspectly .

sections.label: Section 4.1.546
sections.original: Múliebris adhibenda míhi malitia nunc est .
sections.translation: A woman ’ s artfulness must be employed by me .

sections.label: Section 4.1.547
sections.translation: I ’ ll address her .

sections.label: Section 4.1.547b
sections.translation: I ’ ll bring my powers of conversation to bear against him .

sections.label: Section 4.1.548
sections.translation: Health to you !

sections.label: Section 4.1.548b
sections.translation: That health I accept for me and mine .

sections.label: Section 4.1.548c
sections.translation: What besides ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.549
sections.translation: Health to yourself ; what you lent me , I return .

sections.label: Section 4.1.549b
sections.translation: I don ’ t impeach your punctuality .

sections.label: Section 4.1.550
sections.translation: Don ’ t I know you ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.550b
sections.original: Si ego te novi , animum inducam , ut tu noveris .
sections.translation: If I know you , I ’ ll move your feelings , so that you shall know me .

sections.label: Section 4.1.551
sections.translation: Where have I been in the habit of seeing you ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.551b
sections.translation: You are unfairly hard upon me .

sections.label: Section 4.1.551c
sections.translation: Why so ?

sections.label: Section 4.1.551d
sections.translation: Because you think it right that I should be the prompter of your memory .

sections.label: Section 4.1.552b
sections.translation: You speak to the purpose .

sections.label: Section 4.1.553b
sections.translation: You say what ’ s strange to me , Periphanes .

sections.label: Section 4.1.553d
sections.translation: Ah now ! that ’ s better .

sections.label: Section 4.1.554
sections.translation: Do you remember , Philippa —

sections.label: Section 4.1.554b
sections.translation: Yes , I remember that .

sections.label: Section 4.1.554c
sections.translation: At Epidaurus —

sections.label: Section 4.1.554d
sections.translation: Ah ! you have moistened my burning breast with a little drop of comfort .

sections.label: Section 4.1.555b
sections.translation: How I relieved the poverty of you , a poor young maiden , and your mother ?
